serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (500 g) is the standard commercial serving size for this beverage as sold by grab-food vendors in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water; and Beverages, grape drink, canned; composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for grape-aloe Earl Grey tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917), grape juice/drink (FDC ID 167671), and aloe vera gel (FDC ID 2346392) in approximate proportions consistent with the product description (brewed Earl Grey tea base ~60%, grape juice ~30%, aloe vera bits ~10%). Input per_100g values (energy 40 kcal, protein 0.1 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g, sugar 9.0 g, sodium 2 mg, dietary fibre 0.2 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained as the best available estimate. Fat_g is confirmed zero by composite: brewed tea and grape juice both report 0 g fat per USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — grape juice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grape-aloe Earl Grey tea. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for unsweetened grape juice (GI ~52–55, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The tea base contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Aloe vera bits contribute minimal carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (52 × 10.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 5) / 100 = 26.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.0 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; sodium is low at 2 mg/100 g but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this beverage due to its 9.0 g/100 g sugar content, which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; request an unsweetened Earl Grey tea version with fresh grape pieces instead of grape juice concentrate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.0 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g); liquid sugar from grape juice concentrate is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; if desired, request unsweetened Earl Grey tea with a small portion of whole fresh grapes to retain polyphenol benefit while eliminating the rapid glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat and zero trans fat are favourable; however, the high added sugar content (9.0 g/100 g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation risk at regular consumption volumes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the absence of saturated and trans fat is positive, but the high sugar load (45 g per 500 g serving) poses a triglyceride and inflammatory risk; choose unsweetened Earl Grey tea to benefit from bergamot polyphenols and black tea flavonoids without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.0 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; Earl Grey bergamot polyphenols and grape resveratrol/anthocyanins have anti-inflammatory potential that is negated by the sugar load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version to preserve the anti-inflammatory benefit of bergamot polyphenols and grape anthocyanins while avoiding the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grape with Aloe Earl Grey Tea is a chilled, sweetened tea-based beverage sold commercially in Singapore, typically in a 500 ml large cup format. It is prepared by brewing Earl Grey black tea (flavoured with bergamot oil), blending it with grape juice or grape juice concentrate, adding fresh or frozen grape pieces, and incorporating chewy aloe vera gel bits. The drink is served cold and is characterised by a sweet, fruity grape flavour layered over the floral and citrus-bergamot notes of Earl Grey tea, with a mild astringency from the black tea tannins and a soft, gelatinous chew from the aloe vera pieces. Per 100 g, the beverage provides approximately 40 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 10.0 g carbohydrate, 9.0 g sugar, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and 2 mg sodium. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (9.0 g sugar vs 0.2 g fibre per 100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). A standard 500 g serving delivers approximately 200 kcal and 45 g of sugar, which exceeds the WHO daily free-sugar recommendation of 25 g. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glucose absorption from liquid sugar; it is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to the added sugar load despite low sodium. Earl Grey tea contributes bergamot polyphenols and black tea flavonoids with potential anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular-protective properties, but these benefits are substantially offset by the sugar content in the standard commercial preparation. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low) based on grape juice analogy, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 26 per 500 g serving.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific commercial beverage exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed black tea, grape juice, and aloe vera gel in proportions consistent with the product description. The input per_100g values are plausible and consistent with the composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on grape juice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero: both brewed Earl Grey black tea and grape juice report 0.0 g fat per USDA FoodData Central (FDC IDs 171917 and 167671); aloe vera gel also reports 0.0 g fat (FDC ID 2346392). This is a naturally fat-free beverage by ingredient composition. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero for the same reason. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this specific grape-aloe Earl Grey tea blend are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; values would require laboratory analysis of the specific commercial product. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0 as plant-based beverages contain no vitamin B12 or vitamin D by formulation (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for tea and grape juice). Cultural tags in the input JSON incorrectly listed 'Japanese' as primary cuisine and included irrelevant ethnic tags ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers'); these have been corrected to Singaporean cuisine as this is a Singapore-origin commercial beverage. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/); the source has been updated to USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient reference for this composite estimate.
